data: Hey, I’m typewatch. I’m a student with an innate desire for knowledge. I’ve dabbled in various engineering fields along the years, and I built this website to store everything I have done so far.
data.contact-info#Contact Info: Github - typewatch Discord - typewatch Email - typewatch(at)proton(dot)me
data.website#Website:
This website was built with Hugo, is based on a custom theme named Hextra, and is hosted by GitHub.
To learn how to make your own website, check out these resources:
Github Pages Hugo Quickstart Hugo Themes Library

data:
Hey! I’m typewatch, a guy on the internet that likes to learn about interesting stuff. Coincidentally, I also make interesting stuff.
Welcome to my little corner of the internet! I made this website as a companion for my bigger projects; this’ll be where I store and organize writeups, details, resources, docs, guides, and whatnot. I might post a blog entry or two every now and then, too.
More to come soon, I think.

A lot of people are setting up their own home servers to host their own services. Is it actually worth it, or just another pointless hobby that swallows your time and money?
Homelabs, a term for a bundle of or just one home server, are (as the name implies) stay-at-home, always-on computer systems used to run all kinds of services, usually for oneself. You can host a huge variety of services, ranging from your own website or a collaborative Wikipedia (Bookstack) all the way to a private streaming platform (Jellyfin) or a digital document organizer (Paperless-ngx), and many more.
And the best part is you own it all! Any services you host yourself are all yours to manage and modify as you please. Your data stays in your control and not in giant companies or other people’s. And since most server software is open source and free, running all kinds of services for yourself ends up costing basically nothing beyond the power bill for your server.
data.a-comfy-afterwards#A comfy afterwards: Many people say keeping a server up is a huge pain. And maybe it is, but only for the first few days while you’re getting everything configured just right. After that, it’s smooth sailing—I haven’t updated my server in a good two months, and it’s been running seamlessly since
data.conclusion#Conclusion:
Setting up a home server is definitely worth it. Not only do you get to have control over the services you run, you also get to own your data, and be fully independent and non-reliant on big companies who don’t care about the little guy.
That being said, beware, as one old laptop quickly transforms into a lighter wallet and an overkill rackmount system with dedicated networking gear, redundant storage and beefy compute. But it does give something back in the end, so I guess this new piece of equipment’s cost is justified, right?
data.stuff-is-not-looking-good#Stuff is not l:
As of late, many companies—primarily those in the entertainment sector—have been price gouging their customers to see how much they can squeeze out of them. With no other alternative but to stop watching their favorite shows completely, consumers have to begrudgingly accept the price hikes on their subscriptions and hand over the bag to keep enjoying friday movie nights.
This is happening everywhere: Microsoft just can’t stop renaming Office365 raising its prices for basic office software and cloud storage, Spotify is upping the fees on basic tiers, and NordVPN’s pricing keeps going up too.
Other companies try to keep prices stable by lowering service quality or injecting ads everywhere, and that’s not working either.
As you might think, this is very unfair and customers are utterly defenseless against this capitalist anti-consumer behavior. Unfortunately, there’s really nothing you can do about it besides giving up that service entirely. Sure, you can cancel your Netflix subscription and hop to another, cheaper service like Disney+ or AppleTV+, but both of those companies and many more have recently increased their pricing in response to other companies increasing theirs, so you’re ultimately stuck in a never ending loop where companies fight eachother to see who can milk you for more money and you’re left holding the bag (the moneyless bag).
data.the-rabbit-hole-begins#The rabbit hole b: Soon enough, you start looking for new hardware to run more services and spread your server. Eventually, you end up with
data.weaponized-outrage#Weaponized outrage:
Rather than putting up with preying pricing schemes, many very smart people felt such indignation that they set out to build their own streaming service with blackjack and hookers. And make it free for people to host.
Such is the tale of Emby, a selfhosted streaming service platform that lets you stream your own movies and tv shows. Instead of hoping Netflix doesn’t pull your favorite show from it’s catalog, you run the server yourself and access your library from wherever you are.
And such thing happened for countless other services; for streaming there’s Jellyfin, for cloud storage there’s Nextcloud, for images there’s Immich, and so on.
data.youre-on-the-hook#You\u0026rsquo;re on t:
Great power comes with great responsibility; as a sysadmin, you’re tasked with keeping everything running smoothly. That means you’re on the hook if anything breaks and it is your duty to fix or replace it, or your services stop working.
For this, you’re meant to learn a few things to handle a server. But don’t worry—you’ll pick these up naturally as you progress.
I started with an old laptop and an afternoon. After a quick linux install and

data:
I dived head-first into the split keyboard scene, and I loved it. After some months of use, I don’t think I can go back to regular keyboards.
A picture of my half-built lily58. “What is that?” is a question you might be asking yourself after seeing the above image.
That is a lily58—a split ergonomic keyboard designed by kata0510. It is one of the most popular split keyboards out there thanks to its high number of keys (I’ll explain in a bit) and simple build. It’s very similar to a regular keyboard too (compared to other splits), so it’s easier for people who have only interacted with traditional keyboards to transition into the lily58.
After hearing about the hobby through a Discord user praising their Dactyl Manuform (another flavor of split keyboard), I have been doing some research of my own fueled by pure curiosity on what it’s like to use one of these weird-looking keyboards.
What a Dactyl Manuform keyboard looks like. (Via u/Muster7710 on Reddit) Split keyboards are designed around the way human hands naturally move, so instead of forcing both hands onto a single small rectangle, they separate the keyboard into two halves and even rearrange keys to reduce unnecesary finger movement and wrist strain. There are countless split keyboards to pick from, and I chose the lily58.
data.adaptation#Adaptation:
Almost all split keyboards shuffle the physical positions of keys in such a way that you’re forced to rebuild your muscle memory in order to touch type again, no matter if you keep a layout you’re already used to, like the standard QWERTY. In fact, it is often recommended to switch your layout in order to separate muscle memory for standard keyboards and split keyboards.
Luckily, the adaptation period is short, lasting anywhere from a few weeks to never (some people just can’t make the switch over). I personally took around 3 weeks to return to 90% of my original typing speed, and twice that to return to normalcy.
data.comfort#Comfort:
The lily58 is great at making the typing experience more comfortable. Since each half can be placed basically anywhere on your working surface, it means your hands can be positioned as you see fit. For instance, this allows me to spread my arms and straighten my back, keeping my wrists aligned with each keyboard’s half instead of shoving them both together and twisting them to align my fingers with the keys.
On top of that, I also slightly tented my keyboard by 20 degrees. I doubt tenting is doing anything at all, but it makes my keyboard look like an alien artifact so I kept it that way.
data.conclusion#Conclusion:
The lily58 is a staple of the split keyboard community and is considered the de facto gateway drug for wonkier split keyboards. Multiple people praise it for its simple and versatile design, easy adaptation and relatively cheap cost.
However, it is a very niche piece of equipment that might not be right for you. Finding parts and building it is somewhat burdensome, adapting to it takes time, and configuring it requires a little coding knowledge. Deciding if it’s worth making the jump for you depends on how much you appreciate customization on your most used tools and your time.
After my experience dailying a split keyboard, I can’t go back. The lily58 did not fix my shrimp posture, or make me a 10x engineer overnight, but what it did was ruin my opinion of regular keyboards. The switch is easy, but not seamless—every time I return, it makes me aware of the limitations of what is standard, and makes me sad we have not explored anything different just because things are fine the way they are.
Unfortunately, I’ve now become the keyboard equivalent of a wine snob.
data.cost#Cost:
Split keyboards are very niche. Not everyone knows they exist and even less people use them on a daily basis; this makes the split keyboard market not quite diverse.
When buying a split keyboard, you’re left with two choices—you can either buy a DIY kit (all of the necessary parts and components required to build a unit excluding tools and will) or a prebuilt (an already-built and configured unit that arrives ready to use).
Most people tend to sway towards prebuilts of all kinds of products because building something yourself is kind of a hassle. However, the grand majority of split keyboard buyers pick up a DIY kit. Is it because they have more technical competence? Is it because they are nerds and prefer to build something themselves?
No. It is because prebuilts cost a bajillion bucks.
Remember how I said split keyboards aren’t that common? Since there aren’t that many customers, that means there are fewer vendors available, which means fewer parts are ordered, which means fewer technicians assembling them, which means lower product availability. All of that adds up to a $200+ dollar price tag for most prebuilts.
Building is a different story—it’s cheaper money-wise but much more expensive time-wise. Sure, it could be nice to spend an hour or two assembling your keyboard to get acquainted with how it works and save a boatload of money, but once your “hour or two” changes into “by hour five I had something that somewhat resembled a keyboard”, paying someone else to do the dirty work starts sounding like a good idea.
I’m cheap and insane though, so I got a parts kit for $25 and got to work. The build process is quite straightforward—you simply solder over a hundred components perfectly on the first try. Then, I gutted my other keyboard for switches and keycaps, and 3d printed a case and knobs. After a day of combined labor and printing time, I had my keyboard.
data.firmware#Firmware:
Most split keyboards often have custom-built firmware pre-compiled (ready to use) and made available by their own creators. However, the default firmware comes with the most basic of settings and often times keybinds that the creator finds useful, but you might not.
So in most of the cases it is necessary for every user to edit a few lines in the firmware and configure their own keybinds, re-compile firmware, and flash it to their keyboards. And as you might know, nothing says user-friendly quite like having to completely recompile your firmware to apply a caps-lock hotfix.
It took a few days for me to fully dial in all of the keybinds I wanted in my keyboard since I had to closely watch my usage to see what I’d need most, and write it down. Then once I was ready, I sat down and threw an hour away to achieve what I wanted.
Nowadays, some modern keyboard firmware, such as VIA and Vial, is primarily configured via graphical user interfaces, which are much more user-friendly than good ol’ code-based configuration, and usually handle the firmware compiling automatically.
This is now a minor hassle, but it is nevertheless an obstacle that remains present.
data.human-error#Human error:
No person is perfect. I am though, so that’s why I swiftly soldered all 122 components onto my keyboard and immediately got to work without any issues whatsoever.
Then, I realized I forgot to solder one diode the stupid keyboard took a soldering iron and undid one of the diode solder joins out of pure envy of my bulletproof skills. So I had to crack it open and reflow (redo) every single diode’s solder joints on my left half because the ’e’ ky stoppd working outta nowhr and I got rsonably annoyd.
data.layers#Layers:
Layers are alternative keyboard layouts that are only triggered through a combination of keys, usually a modifier key plus another key. An example would be the shift layer, where if you hold down the shift key, you get access to uppercase letters. Layers are not split keyboard exclusive, but they are much more convenient and necessary on split keyboards. There are only so many modifier keys, so split keyboards with more than a certain amount of thumb keys will always have some that are free to use for other purposes, like layer toggle keys. With custom firmware, you can set up your own layer with your most used keys that are accessible in a pinch by holding down or pressing a layer toggle key.
This not only allows people to comfortably make use of cramped sub-40 key configurations, but also turn reasonably big keyboards like the lily58 into efficiency multipliers; as an example, I have a numpad easily accessible on my right half, and arrow keys on the homerow on my left half.
data.nerd-factor#Nerd factor: Split keyboards look kinda dorky. That’s a pretty big con if you ask me.
data.no-really-what-is-that#No really, what i:
The lily58 is not like a traditional keyboard in many ways. It’s split, aligned differently, and has extra stuff like knobs and thumb keys.
To be more precise, it is:
Split: Exactly what it sounds like—instead of one slab, it’s two. This lets you stretch your arms out and keep your wrists straight, making long typing sessions more comfortable. It also has the added benefit of making everyone who walks by your desk think you’re a dweeb ask “Why is it split?” Column staggered: This means that instead of having every row of keys be offset from the others, each column is aligned according to the natural length of your fingers. This way, your fingers follow a more natural range of motion (up and down, curving inward and outward in a straight path) rather than contort and bend sideways to reach keys. Compact: Most split keyboards have around 40 keys or less; with 58 keys, the lily58 is on the bigger side, yet it is still fairly small for a keyboard. Tented: Additionally, you can also tent the keyboard. Tenting is keyboard jargon for angling the keyboard so that it forms a triangle with the table. It lets your hands use the keyboard at a specific angle, keeping your forearm muscles relaxed. This lowers stress placed on the wrists, which some people say makes typing more comfortable. On top of this, the lily58 comes with thumb keys and knobs! You really don’t know what you’re missing until you try it.
Thumb keys fix one big issue with keyboards; thumbs are largely useless in traditional keyboards. Back when the original layout for the traditional keyboard was designed, they decided the best fingers in your hands should be able to press exactly one key. With thumb keys, you can assign more work to your thumbs in the form of any quantity of extra keys. I personally have layer toggles (I’ll get back to it), control, alt and space/enter assigned to the thumb keys of each side.
Knobs on the other hand are already available in a range of traditional keyboards. They’re just normal rotary encoders that let the user provide incremental input, commonly used for volume control. Most keyboards come with just one (they don’t really have much use other than for volume control), but the lily58, being as cool as it is, has a whopping two knobs!
Yes. Both do the same thing.
data.thumb-keys#Thumb keys: Regular keyboards keep thumbs nailed to the spacebar, leaving 8 fingers free for typing. In split keyboards, all ten fingers are put to work, and the lily58 is no exception. With a generous 4 keys per thumb (5 if you include a key that’s a little too far from the general resting position of the thumbs), you can make your workflow smoother by having quicker access to modifier keys through fingers that aren’t needed for anything else.

data:
Over the last few months, Microsoft has been flooring it on their way to enshittify everything they touch. Is it finally time for Linux to shine?
The short answer: No, not really. Linux still has a ways to go, and it is just not ready yet for the average consumer.
The long answer: Windows—historically plagued by bad updates and bloat—has been getting useless AI features hammered into it non-stop since last year.
First came copilot (Microsoft’s in-house multimodal AI model) and along with it preceded a ton of useless AI integrations into every nook and cranny of Windows: if you were to do a fresh install of Windows right now, it will include about a dozen different instances of copilot scattered around almost all major apps, from basic daily-use apps like Edge Browser and the settings app all the way to something as simple as Notepad. It is everywhere.
Starting last year, they even began shipping out computers with a dedicated copilot key for “easy access” to an AI app that was meant to spy on you.
Oh and by the way, you can’t uninstall it. Windows is really picky regarding what system packages you are able to remove, and as such, they make it nearly impossible to delete a feature you don’t want from your own computer. Fortunately, you are able to disable some parts of it temporarily, whatever that means.
All of this fueled a new wave of criticism and overall bad sentiments towards Microsoft and its products, recently gaining it the nickname of “Microslop”. After all of these events, many life-long Windows users looked towards Linux as a simpler alternative.
data.i-dont-like-windows#I don\u0026rsquo;t l:
I have technically been a Linux user for years, having my first taste when I put raspbian on a raspberry pi as a kid. Since then, I’ve tried several distros and have been running Linux on my homelab for a while.
However, I can’t actually say I have used Linux, as I’ve been a Windows person since my first interaction with a computer. I have considered switching before, but I never actually went through with it. Switching’s not hard if you’ve got a good guide and some base knowledge, but it does take some time and preparation—even more so if it’ll be your main OS—which I am too lazy to do. So I put it off, and off again, and then off again again.
And a year or two later, I’ve stopped the perpetual OS migration procrastination. I was already up to my wits end with a number of issues and bugs and nitpicks which Windows has that, albeit minor, make you go crazy over time. But that wasn’t enough to make me go over the trouble of switching. I thought the final straw would be the rollout of AI features, but it seems I was too hesitant to go ahead and spend a whole weekend sorting my files and transfering them over, so I put the switch off again.
The next final straw was when Windows started having tons of system stability issues out of the blue; programs kept hanging, microstutters galore, crashes were aplenty, and even random system-wide freezings occurred for no apparent reason. It was hard to notice at times, but it was still there. I did some digging, and turns out this was caused by the (forced) rollout of a faulty system update. I was mad. This however, was not yet enough for me to switch over.
What it did do was compel me to perform a “tiny experiment” (or so I thought); I had never put Linux on my main computer, so I figured I should just to give it a try. “It’ll take no longer than an hour or two” I thought, so I went ahead with it, and set up a lightweight Arch system on a mini-partition.
Immediately after logging into the DE, something felt off.
It was not the similar yet unfamiliar Plasma environment, nor the different fonts, or the completely unique Linux-only software. It was the smoothness and snappiness of Linux. After working on Windows for so long, I had grown used to the myriad of issues it had; and Linux was a breath of fresh air from the massive disaster that is Windows. This was the real final straw.
That same evening I backed all of my data up, and settled into my distro of choice: Arch Linux.
data.i-switched-to-linux-and-its#I switched t:
Depending on your chosen distro, the switch can be either stupidly simple or akin to rocket surgery.
In the distro I chose, you have two main ways to install the OS onto your computer, and both require quite a bit of manual labor and thinking before you can use your new operating system. You have to specify through the commandline where to install, what to install, how to install, for who to install, and a bunch of other stuff. For an experienced tech, it is a piece of cake, but for the average joe, it can be a little jarring and off-putting.
I had no issues with my installation; with my important files taken care of, all I had to do was plug in a boot thumb drive, do a quick install config and let it do its thing. In less than an hour, I already had a somewhat batteries-included system. I still needed some extra stuff, but it was practically ready-to-use for basic things. Other, more mainstream distros are easier to install and come with even more useful software out of the box. This is great as people short of time or tech knowledge can quickly set up a basic Linux system by themselves and get up and running in no time.
Then comes the bad part; Linux lacks the widespread compatibility that other operating systems like Windows or macOS have out of the box, so you will eventually need to install a couple of drivers to make sure Linux can interact with certain hardware, and then a few more to make sure your hardware runs properly. The download and install of drivers in Linux is a very quick process, but it can take hours of messing around to find exactly what you need and test it. It took around an hour to get my printer working.
After that, you get to tune everything by hand for hours (yay!). Since Linux distros are meant to be installed on all kinds of systems, they have to be built with a catch-all mindset. This means that even if you install the appropriate drivers for Linux to talk to your computer and peripherals, you still have to configure most of them so they work at peak performance with your specific system specs.
For instance, while in Windows my laptop gave me around 4 hours of runtime, Linux out of the box barely gave me 2. However, after taking some measurements and installing a specialized tool for power management, I quintupled my out-of-the-box runtime to 10 hours. The good part about having Linux’s forced fine-tuning is that you can control everything. And when say everything, I really mean everything. I can even disable my GPU now!
And after that, comes the fun part. Well, fun-ish.
Remember how I said you can control everything? You can even change the look of your OS; from fonts and custom colors all the way to system-wide themes. Of course, like all things Linux, it takes a while and you might break something in the process, but in the end you can achieve very nice results.
Finally, you have a complete Linux system. Some parts you can skip, others (usually the worse ones) you can’t. All in all it takes anywhere from an hour to a day or two before you reach the end, but once you do, it feels very nice to have an OS customized to your specific wants and needs, by you. It’s one of those things that brings out a little smile when it works just as you wanted it to.
As for usage, it’s not much different. Yes, you can’t run .exe files the same way you can’t use .dmg files on Windows, you can’t use certain software as it was not designed for Linux, and a couple other things that vary between distros, but you can still do normal computer tasks the same way (or better) with alternative software.
I like it. I won’t be moving OSs any time soon.
data.penguins-are-flightless-birds#Penguins a:
Just as how penguins are really cute birds, but have one major flaw (they can’t fly), Linux is an overall great OS, but has one big issue.
As of 2026, there are still plenty of roadblocks that prevent most users from jumping over. As you might remember, I mentioned “alternative software” in the previous section. What did I mean by this? Oh boy.
For some reason or another, major software corporations like Adobe, Dassault Systémes and Epic Games cannot be bothered to port their fantastic software to Linux. They have done so for multiple Windows and macOS versions, but they just can’t for Linux. This makes for a big bump in the relatively smooth OS transition road, as most users prefer to stick to what they’ve already used to and know works for them. Alternatives exist, but none are perfect copies of other software.
Compatibility has always been a problem in Linux, but fortunately is less widespread nowadays thanks to the development of compatibility layers like Proton (a “translator” software of sorts that allows Windows games to run on Linux) or Wine (an emulator another translator but for Windows apps in general). In some cases, software designed for Windows ends up running better in Linux even through a translation layer.
Then there’s office essentials like Microsoft 365 (Word, PowerPoint, Excel) that cannot run in Linux. Even though some workarounds exist, it is quite difficult to get these working properly. Again, alternatives exist, but they won’t be exactly the same as the original tools.
There are plenty more compatibility problems that all boil down to the software publisher’s fault, and for most conflicts there is no better solution but to switch to another software completely. Despite this, many people have done the switch successfully, and prefer the alternative software (often FOSS, meaning you aren’t locked into it) over their original selection.
data.the-alternative#The alternative:
Actually, there are two alternatives, but both differ in that one lets you reuse your hardware and the other requires you to buy a very expensive machine and pledge allegiance to the fruit. You can guess which one we’ll be skipping today.
When people refer to “Linux”, most mean GNU/Linux. Linux itself is not an OS, but a kernel, which is the baseline layer within an operating system that talks to hardware and manages processes. GNU/Linux is a combination of this kernel plus a bunch of utilities like a shell (a command-line interface and command interpreter), file and text manipulation tools and other stuff.
Then this is divided further into distributions, or “distros”. Every distribution adds or removes software to fit specific use cases, or improve general usability. Modern Linux distros are carefully designed combinations of the kernel, basic GNU utilities, plus a desktop environment and additional applications. Some contain more software than others by default, but it all depends on that specific distro’s use case and ideology.
Linux is one of the most prevalent examples of open source software and collaboration. Originally created by Linus Torvalds, it is now maintained by multiple users throughout the world. The grand majority of distributions follow this mindset, being FOSS (free and open source software) oriented.
It has been the dominant OS on the enterprise sector, running in most servers and supercomputers, but it just hasn’t gotten its time in the consumer spotlight.
Despite its dominance outside the consumer sector, for years Linux has been trying to increase its stake in the consumer OS market. Unfortunately, it hasn’t been able to gain more than 5% of the consumer desktop OS market since its appearance. This is fantastic for such niche software, but 5% isn’t enough to be competitive with bigger sharks like Windows’ menacing 65% and macOS’s 15%.
Linux has not been around in the market as long as others have, and isn’t as widely recognized, but the public opinion on it has been growing stronger every year, and with recent improvements it finally looks like it might just be about time for it to claim more territory.
data.why-you-should-switch#Why you should swi:
My reasons to switch were already there for a long time, and the sentiment too. All it took was one last thing to switch over.
It’s different for everyone, but I can almost guarantee you already have one or more reasons to hate Windows. And if not—trust me—you will. The community agrees that modern Linux distros are very flexible and can be used by anyone given sufficient time to get the gist of things, but for people like old gramps that just wants to check his Facebook once a month, it may not be the best pick yet.
Windows has been getting significantly worse over time as of late. If you’re able to make the switch, you should go for it; maybe not towards a complex distro like Arch if you don’t feel very comfortable with setting everything up from scratch or using the command-line, but towards a simpler, more streamlined one like Fedora or Ubuntu, which come with plenty of batteries out of the box and have a fancy graphical installer.
Linux may not be the best, but the tradeoff is definitely worth it for the customizability, stability and pure freedom it provides. With alternative operating systems losing scope and falling for profiteering over user comfort, Linux is one of, if not the best option for an OS out there.

/blog/linuxstillsucks/.title: Linux still sucks—here's why you should use it

data: Welcome to my docs archive; here you can find in-depth information regarding almost all of my projects.
data.feedback#Feedback: I heavily appreciate feedback; please visit the about page for more information.

data:
Driver40 Rev1 GitHub Driver40 is an ultraportable, 4x5 ortholinear, low profile split keyboard. Handful of words huh? Here’s what they mean:
4x5: The keyboard is built with a 4-row, 5-column key configuration. ortholinear: This means that the keys in the keyboard are not staggered, and instead aligned in a square grid. low profile: As you might’ve guessed, this means that the keyboard’s height is low, or in other words, it is a particularly slim keyboard. split: In contrast to a typical keyboard, which is one long rectangular slab with keys, a split keyboard is two slabs, interconnected with wires or through wireless signals.
data.the-process#The Process: Building a fully custom keyboard is surprisingly easy nowadays; with just a little bit of tech-wiz and a weekend or two, you can make your very own keyboard.
data.why#Why?:
I built a lily58 not very long ago, and I love it. It’s a 58-key, 4x6 column staggered split keyboard.
It’s really comfortable to type on for long periods of time, and I found the way it uses layers to “hide” keys that are barely used but still necessary to keep at hand much more practical than having a big keyboard with keys for all kinds of functions.
As a student, I spend most of my time away from my desk: sometimes I’m on campus, other times I’m at a coffee shop, maybe I’m at a friend’s, or at the library—and unfortunately, the homework doesn’t stop flowing. Thanks to this I pretty much always carry my school backpack with me to work on the go—freeing time for other stuff, like my hobbies.
With time, I’ve refined my perfect layout on the lily. I have come to the realization that—something that I saw as shocking and painful to use—whoever was the first to pave the way into <40% keyboards had a genius idea. With layers, less keys is possible and actually better (YMMV) than more keys. You can stash everything you’d ever need into a couple of layers you can access with a combo of thumb cluster keys and have all of your regular functions, cherry-picked for efficiency, literally at your fingertips.
Miss the F-row? Press thumbkey1 and get it back alongside a numrow and symbols or whatever.
Want a numpad? Done, it’s in layer 3 on your right half of the keyboard. Hold thumbkey1 + thumbkey2 to access it.
Given this, I was able to go for a radical 40-key layout without sacrificing anything but whatever time it’d take to learn that keyboard’s layout.
Enter driver40, a keyboard inspired by Horiguchi Hidetaka’s conductor keyboard. When I stumbled upon conductor, I really liked its design and the bells and whistles attatched to it, but I thought I could go further with its concept, and fine-tune it to my needs.

data:
Note
Sourcing links are for reference. They may not be the exact parts I used. Please report broken links.
Important
PCB, case, plate and wiring designs shown here may not represent the newest revisions.

Note
Before you begin, make sure you have everything you need (tools, parts, guides, snacks) at hand. It’ll make the job much easier.
Important
PCB, case, plate and wiring designs shown here may not represent the newest revisions.
Warning
This project involves wiring. Please be careful when handling electrical equipment, and make sure to properly test your parts before using them.
Tools Required Screwdriver Tweezers Soldering Iron Solder & Flux Flashlight Magnifying Glass
data.step-1#Step 1:
Solder all 40 diodes into their corresponding pads, marked as such:
The line on your diode should face the line on the marking. Tip
To make installation easier, pre-tin the pads, align the diode, and while holding it down with tweezers, use your iron to reflow each leg into place.
data.step-2#Step 2:
Solder all 40 hotswap sockets into their marked spots, which look like the following:
The sockets should fit into the hole cutouts on the footprints. Tip
Same as with the diodes: Pre-tin the pads with as much solder as you can before spilling. Then, align the socket with the holes and pads, and press down on either side with your iron. Hold until the solder melts, and firmly press the socket down into the pad.
data.step-3#Step 3:
Solder headers in place.
Insert each strip into the corresponding holes, and solder them onto the board through the bottom. Note
Make sure to push them all the way in. If they are misaligned, the MCU will not fit.
data.step-4#Step 4:
Mount the MCU atop the already installed headers, and solder it in place.
Caution
Make sure to keep the MCU flush with the headers, or you risk cracking it while soldering it in place.

data: Totum 2WD 24V v0.1 GitHub Totum has been my primary project over the last few months. It is unfinished.

Zenith Base v1.1 GitHub Zenith is a product development exercise and a nifty 3D printer exhaust filtering system.
It previously was intended to be a whole-room purifier with smart capabilities, but it was scrubbed in favor of the simpler yet more necessary 3D printer implementation.
data.specs#Specs:
I haven’t tested Zenith properly yet, nor do I have the necessary equipment, but when I do I’ll post the sweet sweet data here. For now, you get some basic technical specifications:
Runs on 24 VDC Uses MERV filters ~40-45 CFM ~2 mmH2O ~26 dB

data:
Note
Sourcing links are for reference. They may not be the exact parts I used. Please report broken links.
data.electronics#Electronics:
Zenith uses barely any electronics, and they are the exact same for both versions.
Part Amount Source Price per Unit Total Price 120mm pc fan 2 link $5 $10 24V step-down converter 1 link $5 $5
data.filter-media#Filter Media: Try to find non-acidic activated charcoal. The acidic stuff will rust the inside of your printer in a pinch.
data.filters#Filters:
You can use pretty much any filter you want and cut it to size. I recommend you steer clear of higher MERV ratings because while they do their job perfectly fine, they impair airflow and thus lower your overall filtering capability.
I used MERV10 filters I got from Home Depot and they seem to work fine.
data.hardware#Hardware:
Both versions use the same hardware and in equal amounts. Any head type is fine unless explicitly stated.
Part Amount Source Price per Unit Total Price M3x10 countersunk 10 link $0.20 $2 M3x15 sockethead 15 link $0.20 $3
data.structure#Structure:
Depending on your configuration, you will need a number of printed parts in order to build the outer structure of your filter.
Base Zenith Base is the original configuration designed to filter particulates. This version features a two-filter one-fan assembly and requires the least number of parts.
(x4) Base Wall (x2) Base Grill (x1) Mount Combo Zenith Combo is equipped to filter particulates and VOCs. This version is slightly bigger than the original to house the necessary carbon filter media and refilling mechanism.
(x4) Combo Wall (x1) Combo Grill (x1) Door (x1) Mount

data:
Note
Before you begin, make sure you have everything you need (tools, parts, guides, snacks) at hand. It’ll make the job much easier.
Warning
This project involves wiring. Please be careful when handling electrical equipment, and make sure to properly test your parts before using them.
Tools Required Screwdriver Pliers Soldering Iron
These are some of the things I've worked on Driver40 Totum Zenith
